Faq_parent_1: ❓ Questions Parents Ask Us All the Time
Faq_parent_2: What age can my child start learning to code?
Faq_parent_3: CO-DE Academy welcomes kids from age 4, starting with Design Thinking to build logical thinking skills first.
Faq_parent_4: What's the difference between Scratch and Python — and which one should my child start with?
Faq_parent_5: Scratch is block-based coding, best for kids aged 6 and up. Python is text based and better suited for kids from age 9 who are ready to start writing real code.
Faq_parent_6: What does my child actually get out of learning to code?
Faq_parent_7: Beyond coding itself, kids build problem-solving skills, logical thinking, and creativity — three of the most important skills for the 21st century.
Blogs_Scratch_2: How to Start Learning Scratch and Have Fun Doing It
Blogs_Scratch_3: Never heard of Scratch before? That's totally fine. By the end of this article, you'll have gone from zero to building your first real project on Scratch — and it'll take you less than 8 minutes.
Blogs_Scratch_5: Scratch is a coding platform for kids, built by MIT. Instead of typing code, kids drag and drop blocks to build things such as games, animations, stories, you name it. It's free at scratch.mit.edu, works right in the browser, and there's nothing to install. Kids as young as 6 can jump straight in.
Blogs_Scratch_16: changes appearance and makes characters speak
Blogs_Scratch_18: Open your browser and go to
Blogs_Scratch_21: on the homepage. You can start making projects right away without signing up. (You can always create an account later if you want to save your work.)
Blogs_Scratch_22: Get to know the Scratch screen
Blogs_Scratch_23: Before doing anything else, try clicking the blue block called:
Blogs_Scratch_24: The orange cat on the screen will move right away.
Blogs_Scratch_25: That’s coding — it’s really that simple.
Blogs_Scratch_26: Build your first program in 5 minutes
Blogs_Scratch_27: Drag the code blocks to make the cat walk in a square shape, then click the green flag at the top. The cat will start moving automatically right away.
Blogs_Scratch_28: 💡 Try this: change the number in “move 10 steps” to 50
Blogs_Scratch_29: Then click the green flag again — notice the difference? That’s what “editing code” looks like
Blogs_Scratch_31: At the bottom-right corner of the screen, you’ll see the “Choose a Sprite” and “Choose a Backdrop” buttons. Click them to pick any character or background you like — there are hundreds to choose from.
Blogs_Scratch_33: Try making the cat move in a star shape using the “Point in direction” block. If you get stuck, ask your teacher for a little help.
Blogs_Scratch_35: What age can kids start learning Scratch?
Blogs_Scratch_36: Kids can start as young as 6 years old. No English or coding background is needed.
Blogs_Scratch_37: Do they need coding experience first?
Blogs_Scratch_38: Not at all. Scratch is designed especially for beginners.
Blogs_Scratch_39: What’s the difference between online classes and learning at a center?
Blogs_Scratch_40: Online classes are convenient and can be done from home. In-person classes give kids more direct interaction with the teacher.
Blogs_Scratch_41: Ready for your child to start with Scratch?
Blogs_Scratch_42: At CO-DE Academy, we offer Scratch courses for kids aged 6 and up. Classes are project based, so students learn by building real projects in every session. We offer both online classes and in-person learning in Bangkok.
Blogs_Scratch_45: Our teachers understand kids — not just code 💛
Blogs_Parent_2: 5 Things to Check Before Enrolling Your Child in a Coding School
Blogs_Parent_21: Choose Wrong and You're Wasting Both Money and Time
Blogs_Parent_3: Finding a coding school for kids isn't hard. A quick Google search gives you dozens of options. But how do you actually know which one teaches well — and which one just has a good-looking website?
Blogs_Parent_5: It's about building the foundation for your child to think logically, solve problems, and be ready for whatever career the future brings. Here are five things worth asking before you decide.
Blogs_Parent_9: 📋 A quick checklist before you decide
Blogs_Parent_10: ✅ Teacher has a Computer Science degree or equivalent background
Blogs_Parent_11: ✅ Teaches kids to think — not just to copy
Blogs_Parent_13: ✅ Curriculum is clearly separated by age group
Blogs_Parent_14: ✅  Child walks away with real work to show for it
Blogs_Parent_15: Does the teacher actually have a background in this field?
Blogs_Parent_16: A good coding teacher needs two things at once — real knowledge of computer science, and the ability to teach kids. Neither one alone is enough.
Blogs_Parent_17: Ask directly whether the teacher graduated in Computer Engineering, Computer Science, or a related field. A teacher with a solid foundation can explain complex logic accurately — not just repeat a method they memorised.
Blogs_Parent_18: But here's what's even harder than knowing the subject:
Blogs_Parent_20: There's no single correct way to write code. One child might solve something one way, the teacher another way, and both get the same output. A good teacher needs to explain what makes each approach stronger or weaker — not just say "follow the answer key." Telling a child there's only one right way is the fastest way to shut down their thinking.
Blogs_Parent_22: Are they teaching kids to think — or just to copy?
Blogs_Parent_23: A lot of parents assume that coding for kids means building robots or making games. That's not wrong — but those are outputs, not the actual content.
Blogs_Parent_26: — understanding how code works, thinking in algorithms, understanding syntax, breaking big problems into smaller steps, and recognising patterns that can be reused to solve problems.
Blogs_Parent_27: A child who genuinely understands these principles can pick up any new coding language on their own, for the rest of their life. A good school doesn't let kids just copy code. It makes sure they understand why that code works.
Blogs_Parent_28: Can kids have fun — even when the content is hard?
Blogs_Parent_29: Coding is genuinely complex. But when it's taught well, kids don't feel that weight at all. The real sign that a school is doing it right isn't just that kids can follow instructions — it's that kids feel
Blogs_Parent_30: proud of what they built themselves.
Blogs_Parent_31: That sense of pride is what brings them back. When a child is having fun, they want to keep learning on their own. A good school doesn't just teach kids to write code — it makes them fall in love with programming.
Blogs_Parent_32: Is the curriculum actually designed for your child's age?
Blogs_Parent_33: Children develop very differently at each stage. A good curriculum has a clear learning path — it doesn't put every age group in the same class.
Blogs_Parent_34: Ages 4–5 should start with Design Thinking and unplugged coding, building systematic thinking without a computer. From age 6, kids are ready for Scratch and visual programming — dragging blocks helps them understand logic without needing to type. From age 9 and up, they can move into textbased coding like Python or Roblox game development, where they write real code.
Blogs_Parent_35: If a coding school tells you they take all ages in the same class, that's a red flag.
Blogs_Parent_36: What does your child actually walk away with?
Blogs_Parent_37: A certificate matters a lot less than real work. Your child should leave with a project they built themselves — a game people can actually play, an animation they can share, or a program they can show their friends. That's the proof that they learned something real, not just sat through a class.
Blogs_Parent_38: The pride a child feels when they see their own program running — that's the best motivation to keep going.
Blogs_Parent_39: 🎉 Tick all five — and you've probably found the right place.
Blogs_Parent_40: Want to find the right fit for your child?
Blogs_Parent_43: At CO-DE Academy, we offer a free trial class — no cost, no commitment. Parents get to see exactly how we teach. Kids get to build their first real project. Every class is led by teachers with a Computer Science background who understand how children at each age actually learn.
Blogs_Parent_44: 🎯 Let us help you find the right course.
Blogs_Parent_45: 🌟 We don't teach kids to memorize. We teach them to think — and to build. 🌟

blogs_5: Why Kids Should Learn Python? What ever Parent Needs to Know
blogs_6: Python is the coding language most suited for children, because its structure reads almost like plain English. Kids as young as 9 can start learning without needing any prior experience — and they can see real results from day one.
Tab_Parent_2: This collection of articles, techniques, and guidelines for choosing coding courses helps Parents understand the learning style of modern children.
blogs_Recommend_1: Why Kids Should Start Learning to
blogs_Recommend_3: Technology shapes almost every part of life today — how we work, how we learn, and how we solve problems. That's why coding has become one of the most valuable skills a child can develop early on. And when it comes to choosing a first programming language, Python keeps coming out on top. So what makes Python such a great fit for kids? And why are more and more parents across Thailand starting their children on this path while they're still young? Here's what you need to know.
blogs_Recommend_4: Python Is Genuinely Easy to Pick Up
blogs_Recommend_5: One of the biggest hurdles for young learners is feeling like coding is too complicated or technical. Python removes that barrier. Its syntax reads almost like plain English, which means kids can focus on what they're trying to build rather than wrestling with confusing rules. That sense of clarity matters — it keeps children from getting discouraged and gives them the confidence to keep going.
blogs_Recommend_7: There's something powerful about writing a few lines of code and watching something actually happen. With Python, kids can build simple games, small apps, or handy little programs almost immediately. That instant feedback loop — write it, run it, see it work — is one of the best motivators in learning. It turns abstract concepts into something real and fun.
blogs_Recommend_8: It's the Language the Real World Uses
blogs_Recommend_9: Python isn't just a 'beginner's language' that kids outgrow. It's the same language used by Google, Netflix, NASA, and countless other organizations for everything from AI and data science to web development and automation. When a child learns Python, they're not just picking up a skill for school — they're building a foundation that directly connects to where careers in technology are headed.
blogs_Recommend_10: It Teaches Kids How to Think
blogs_Recommend_11: Coding isn't only about computers. Learning to write programs trains the brain to break down problems, think in sequences, and work through challenges step by step. These logical thinking skills carry over into every subject — maths, science, even everyday decision-making. It's one of the reasons educators and child development experts increasingly recommend coding as a core part of a well-rounded education
blogs_Recommend_13: Children who start learning Python young tend to develop a meaningful head start. By the time they reach secondary school or university, they're already comfortable with concepts their peers are just beginning to encounter. That advantage matters — whether they go on to study computer science, pursue a career in tech, or one day build something of their own.
blogs_Recommend_14: Not Sure Where Your Child Stands? Start with a Free Assessment
blogs_Recommend_15: If you're wondering what level your child is at right now, CO-DE Academy offers a free Python knowledge assessment designed specifically for kids. It's a simple, structured way to understand their current skills and find the right learning path for their age and experience.
blogs_Recommend_18: At CO-DE Academy, we believe coding is the skill that defines the next generation. Our Python courses for children are built around project-based learning — meaning kids don't just study concepts, they build things. Along the way, they develop both technical skills and the kind of confidence and creative thinking that lasts well beyond any single class.
